%%
%% The abstract is a short summary of the work to be presented in the
%% article.
\begin{abstract}
Robotic manipulation sits at the intersection of perception, reasoning, and physical interaction, making it one of the most demanding and consequential problems in embodied intelligence. Recent advances in vision-language-action models, world models, and diffusion-based policies have substantially expanded the design space, yet the gap between benchmark performance and reliable deployment persists — a sign that manipulation intelligence is shaped not by model architecture alone, but by the interplay among embodiment constraints, data regimes, learning design, and deployment requirements.
This survey provides a systems-oriented analysis of learned manipulation intelligence for robotic arms. We organize the field around four interdependent stages. First, we introduce a three-tier data regime taxonomy and analyze the acquisition, synthesis, refinement, and pipeline infrastructure that produces training data at scale. Second, we decompose the learning stack into cognitive planning and policy learning, classify their coupling into three integration architectures, and show how data regime choices constrain architectural options. Third, we analyze a three-stage deployment lifecycle and identify how preparation, adaptation, and inference objectives fail to compose cleanly in practice. Fourth, we synthesize evaluation methodology across task quality, data efficiency, generalization, and deployment cost, and identify structural gaps that current metrics leave unaddressed.
Across these stages, a consistent pattern emerges: choices made upstream — in embodiment, data collection, and architectural design — propagate as constraints downstream, and evaluation frameworks that treat these stages independently cannot capture the trade-offs that matter most for real-world deployment. We conclude with open challenges in heterogeneous data transfer, cross-embodiment generalization, and system-level trustworthiness.
\end{abstract}

\section{Introduction}\label{sec:introduction}

Embodied intelligence views cognition as arising from the dynamic coupling of brain, body, and environment, rather than as a purely disembodied computational process~\cite{clarkBeingTherePutting1998, brooksIntelligenceWithoutRepresentation1991}.
Robotic manipulation provides a particularly challenging and revealing setting for examining this view, as it requires joint reasoning over contact dynamics, force exchange, high-DoF kinematics, and fine-grained object perception~\cite{khatibInertialPropertiesRobot1987}.
These characteristics have made robotic manipulation a central focus of embodied intelligence research, a position further reinforced by the rise of deep learning and, more recently, the large-scale emergence of foundation models and LLMs. 
Among all manipulation platforms, robotic arms remain the most mature and widely deployed, making them the primary subject of this survey.

Recent advances in vision-language-action models, world models, and diffusion-based policies have rapidly expanded the design space of robotic manipulation. 
At the same time, they have made clear that manipulation performance is not determined by model architecture alone, but by the interaction among embodiment, data production, learning design, deployment constraints, and evaluation practice. 
Existing surveys cover important parts of this landscape from different perspectives, including unified planning--control abstractions for robot manipulation~\cite{baiUnifiedUnderstandingRobot2025}, 
foundation-model components in real-world robotics~\cite{kawaharazukaVisionLanguageActionModelsRobotics2025}, 
object-centric embodied learning for robotic manipulation~\cite{survey2025objectcentric}, 
and broader embodied AI systems driven by LLMs and world models~\cite{liuEmbodiedIntelligenceSynergy2025}. 
However, these works either remain more method-centric, focus on particular model families, or operate at a broader level of abstraction. 
In contrast, we treat embodied manipulation as a system problem and examine its stages through a unified, dependency-aware framework. 
Concretely, we introduce taxonomies for data regimes, cognitive planning, policy learning, and system integration, using them to clarify recurring trade-offs and design dependencies across the manipulation pipeline.

Arrows indicate conceptual dependencies across the survey structure.}\label{fig:survey_structure}
\end{figure}

\textbf{Objectives and Scope of This Survey:}
This survey examines learned manipulation intelligence across robotic arm platforms, including stationary, bimanual, mobile, and humanoid configurations, with emphasis on how embodiment, data, learning, deployment, and evaluation interact as a system.

\textbf{Contributions:}
This survey makes three contributions.
First, this survey first introduces a layered taxonomy of embodied datasets, and then analyzes the current data production ecosystem in depth, showing how acquisition settings, simulation environments, and large-scale data pipelines collectively generate and sustain the datasets and benchmarks used in modern robotic manipulation.
Second, it separates cognitive planning and policy learning, and analyzes how different learning paradigms fit into this decomposition, how they are integrated into complete manipulation systems, and what trade-offs they entail for deployment.
Third, it treats embodied manipulation as a system problem, analyzing how embodiment constraints, data regimes, learning architectures, deployment requirements, and evaluation protocols are mutually coupled, and how these components jointly shape system behavior and real-world performance.

\textbf{Structure of the Paper:}
Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_hardware} reviews physical embodiment and software infrastructure. Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset} examines the data ecosystem from datasets through acquisition, synthesis, refinement, and pipelines. Section~\ref{sec:embodiment_method} analyzes cognitive planning, policy learning, integration models, and deployment. Section~\ref{sec:evaluation} synthesizes evaluation methodologies, and Section~\ref{sec:challenges} concludes with open challenges and future directions. Figure~\ref{fig:survey_structure} summarizes this organization.

\section{System Architecture of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:embodiment_hardware}

The action space of a robotic system is constrained by its hardware embodiment. End-effector geometry, manipulator kinematics, and base mobility together determine what tasks a policy can physically execute. This section reviews the hardware landscape through end effectors, stationary platforms, and mobile platforms, and then examines the software stack that closes the loop from perception to actuation.

\subsection{Physical Embodiment}

The physical embodiment of a manipulation system spans three levels of abstraction (Fig.~\ref{fig:embodiment_taxonomy}): the \emph{end effector} that mediates contact, the \emph{stationary platform} that provides kinematic reach and torque bandwidth, and the \emph{mobile platform} that extends the workspace to unstructured environments.

\begin{figure}[t]
    \centering
    \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=12cm 4.3cm 6cm 3.5cm,clip]{assets/embodiment.pdf}
    \caption{Taxonomy of physical embodiments for robotic manipulation. End effectors are classified by contact modality (jaw gripper, dexterous hand, soft hand) and sensing (vision, tactile); platforms are divided into stationary (single-arm, bimanual) and mobile (wheeled, quadrupedal, humanoid).}
    \Description{A hierarchical diagram showing the taxonomy of physical embodiments for robotic manipulation. End effectors are classified by contact modality (jaw gripper, dexterous hand, soft hand) and sensing (vision, tactile); platforms are divided into stationary (single-arm, bimanual) and mobile (wheeled, quadrupedal, humanoid).}\label{fig:embodiment_taxonomy}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection{End Effectors and Contact Sensing}\label{subsec:end_effectors}

End effectors determine how the robot makes contact with the world, while contact sensing determines what the policy can observe once that contact begins. Parallel-jaw and adaptive grippers, including common Robotiq 2F/3F-style designs, are widely used when grasp robustness and implementation simplicity matter more than in-hand dexterity. Dexterous hands such as Shadow Hand and Allegro Hand enlarge the reachable manipulation space, but they also increase action dimensionality and make contact planning, inverse kinematics, and force distribution more difficult. Soft hands such as RBO Hand~3~\cite{Puhlmann_2022}, Festo BionicSoftHand, and SpiRobs shift part of this burden from explicit force control to mechanical compliance, improving tolerance to uncertainty while making state estimation and dynamics modeling more difficult.

The sensing stack largely determines whether contact-rich control can remain closed loop. Eye-in-hand vision resolves object geometry before contact, but it provides only indirect information about force, slip, and local deformation once contact occurs. Tactile sensing fills this gap: vision-based tactile sensors such as GelSight~\cite{zhao2023gelsightsveltehumanfingershaped} and DIGIT convert contact geometry into images, while force- and vibration-sensitive sensors such as BioTac provide signals for slip detection and contact regulation. In practice, visuo-tactile integration matters most in insertion, re-grasping, and fragile-object handling, where task-relevant feedback comes primarily from contact state.

\subsubsection{Fixed-base Platforms}\label{subsec:manipulators}

Fixed-base manipulators are still the most common platform for contact-rich manipulation because they provide stable control conditions and separate manipulation from mobility. Single-arm systems such as Franka Panda, KUKA iiwa, and Kinova Gen3 are widely used for policy benchmarking, teleoperation, and high-precision force-controlled tasks. Their practical advantage is that base stability is largely fixed, so the control problem can focus on arm kinematics, contact regulation, and end-effector behavior.

Bimanual fixed-base systems, such as dual Franka Panda setups or ABB YuMi, expand the task set to handover, folding, assembly, and other coordinated interactions, but they introduce stronger coupling between arms. Once two manipulators act on the same object, the control problem must account for internal-force regulation, collision avoidance, and occasional closed-chain constraints. Relative to single-arm setups, these platforms shift the bottleneck from local end-effector control to coordination across multiple contact points.

\subsubsection{Mobile and Whole-body Platforms}\label{subsec:mobile_platforms}

Mounting manipulators on mobile bases expands task coverage, but it also couples manipulation to system-level stability and motion planning. Wheeled mobile manipulators such as PR2, TIAGo, and Stretch combine arm control with base navigation, forcing the controller to reconcile base constraints with end-effector accuracy. Legged manipulators, for example Spot-based systems or Unitree platforms equipped with arms, add a stronger form of coupling: when the arm interacts with the environment, contact forces affect foothold stability, and manipulation therefore competes directly with locomotion and balance.

Humanoids such as Atlas, Figure~02, Optimus, and Unitree G1 combine bipedal balance, whole-body coordination, and often bimanual manipulation within a single control stack. In these systems, manipulation is no longer a localized arm problem: contact forces, posture regulation, center-of-mass motion, and angular momentum must all be coordinated together. As embodiment scope expands from fixed-base arms to mobile and humanoid systems, reachable task diversity increases, but so does the degree of coupling among contact, motion, and stability.

\subsection{Software Framework}

\subsubsection{Modern Robotics Middleware and Operating Systems}\label{subsec:control_middleware}

We structure the software stack into three tiers (Table~\ref{tab:framework_comparison}): the Kernel Layer (L0) for hard real-time motor control, the Transport Layer (L1) for inter-process data flow, and the System Layer (L2) for distributed orchestration.

The kernel layer (L0) centers on temporal determinism. Industrial stacks rely on kernels such as VxWorks\footnote{\url{https://www.windriver.com/products/vxworks}} and QNX\footnote{\url{https://blackberry.qnx.com}} for bounded interrupt latency and fault isolation, while Zephyr\footnote{\url{https://zephyrproject.org/}} extends the same guarantees to resource-constrained edge devices.

The transport layer (L1) has consolidated around ROS~2 as the dominant communication backbone, with decentralized DDS replacing earlier centralized designs~\cite{macenski2022robot}. For embodied models, however, the bottleneck is increasingly not connectivity itself but the cost of moving multimodal data, which has driven zero-copy dataflow designs such as Dora-rs~\cite{zhang2026dora}.

The system layer (L2) shifts the emphasis from message passing to orchestration across robots, edge devices, clouds, and fleets. Systems such as Zenoh-Flow and FogROS2 treat deployment, routing, and compute offloading as first-class concerns, while RoboOS represents a more recent AI-native direction in which high-level multimodal planning is coupled to reusable skill libraries across heterogeneous embodiments~\cite{baldoni2023zenohflow, ichnowski2022fogros2, wang2025roboos}.

L0 denotes hard real-time kernels for microsecond-level motor-control determinism. L1 denotes middleware for high-throughput, low-latency inter-process communication. L2 denotes orchestration frameworks for distributed deployment, fleet management, and AI-native coordination. Performance refers to interrupt or IPC latency for L0/L1 systems, and to deployment granularity or orchestration level for L2 systems.}\label{tab:framework_comparison}

\subsubsection{Low-level Motion Planning and Control}

While middleware provides the communication infrastructure, the low-level motion planning and control layer turns task-space intents into physically executable joint commands. Its central burden is to satisfy several coupled constraints at once: mapping between operational and joint space, compensating nonlinear rigid-body dynamics, respecting joint and collision limits, and remaining stable under external contact. For this reason, modern embodied systems increasingly rely on optimization-based controllers, especially model predictive control and whole-body control, which solve tracking, feasibility, and safety within a single constrained problem updated at control rate~\cite{schulman2014optimizing}.

In manipulation, contact behavior is as important as free-space accuracy. Impedance control remains a standard mechanism for shaping compliant interaction during assembly, tool use, and human--robot collaboration, because it lets the robot absorb deviations through compliance and tuned stiffness. In practice, the effectiveness of MPC, WBC, and impedance control still depends on L0-level temporal determinism: timing jitter in the execution loop can destabilize high-frequency feedback long before perception or learning becomes the limiting factor.

Together, hardware embodiment and the software stack define the physical and computational constraints under which any learned policy must operate. The next question is what data is required to learn effectively under these constraints, and how that data should be structured, collected, and scaled.

\section{Data Ecosystem of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:embodiment_dataset}

Data is central to embodied manipulation, but its role extends beyond the datasets used for training. In practice, progress depends equally on how manipulation data is collected, refined, and scaled across tasks, scenes, and embodiments. Figure~\ref{fig:data_support} summarizes this broader data support framework.

Accordingly, this chapter moves from embodied datasets to the infrastructures that produce and improve them. We begin with dataset regimes in \S\ref{sec:eai_datasets}, then examine data acquisition in \S\ref{sec:data_acquisition}, data refinement in \S\ref{sec:data_refinement}, and finally the large-scale pipelines that integrate these processes in \S\ref{sec:data_factories_robotic_arms}.

\subsection{Embodied Datasets}\label{sec:eai_datasets}

Embodied datasets provide the foundation for manipulation learning. Their key differences lie in the type of supervision and knowledge they contribute to downstream systems. We therefore organize existing datasets into three tiers according to the dominant knowledge regime they expose (Tables~\ref{tab:grasping_datasets}--\ref{tab:foundation_datasets}). Skill-level datasets ($\mathcal{K}_1$) densely sample local interaction phenomena such as grasp geometry, contact patterns, and material response. Supervision targets single atomic actions: whether a grasp succeeds, how a push affects object motion, or which contact forces arise during insertion. Evaluation is therefore tied to individual actions. Task-level datasets ($\mathcal{K}_2$) shift supervision to multi-step sequences indexed by natural language instructions. Here the relevant outcome is overall instruction completion across the trajectory. This requires learning how individual skills work, when they should be invoked, and in what order they should be composed. Foundation-level datasets ($\mathcal{K}_3$) shift supervision again, now targeting transfer across robot morphologies. The primary objective becomes whether a policy generalizes to unseen embodiments under heterogeneous sensors and action spaces. Taken together, these tiers describe an ordered progression from local interaction grounding to task composition and then to cross-embodiment generalization. We use the shorthand $\mathcal{K}_i \approx \mathcal{K}_{i-1} \oplus \mathcal{D}_i$ to denote that each tier augments the previous one with new supervision, diversity, and transfer demands.

\textbf{DexGraspNet 2.0}~\cite{zhang2024dexgraspnet20learninggenerative} & 2024 & Dexterous Grasp & Multi-Object & 5,355 & Sim & 427M grasps & Point Cloud & $\times$ \\
\textbf{Dex1B}~\cite{ye2025dex1b} & 2025 & Dexterous Grasp & Single Object & 1M & Sim & 1B grasps & Point Cloud & $\times$ \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{9}{c}{\textbf{Language-Grounded Grasping}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Grasp-Anything}~\cite{vuong2023graspanything} & 2023 & Rect.\ Grasp + Push & Multi-Object & 3M & Syn & 1M imgs (600M grasps) & RGB & $\checkmark$ \\
\textbf{ReasoningGrasp}~\cite{jin2024reasoninggrasp} & 2024 & 6-DoF Grasp & Multi-Object & 64 & Real & 99M grasps & RGB-D & $\checkmark$ \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{9}{c}{\textbf{Diverse Motor Primitives}} \\
\midrule
\textbf{Omnipush}~\cite{bauza2021omnipush} & 2019 & Pushing (Planar) & Single Object & 250 & Real & 62.5k pushes & RGB-D & $\times$ \\
\textbf{PartNet-Mobility}~\cite{xiang2020sapien} & 2020 & Articulated (Open/Close) & Single Object & 2,346 models & Sim & 2,346 articulated assets & 3D / PC & $\times$ \\
\textbf{FurnitureBench}~\cite{heo2023furniturebench} & 2023 & Assembly (Precise) & Tabletop & Furniture & Sim+Real & 5.1k demos (220 hrs) & RGB & $\times$ \\

\end{tabular}
}
\end{table}

Skill-level datasets record atomic motor primitives and their local interaction dynamics. Table~\ref{tab:grasping_datasets} lists representative datasets along this tier.
Early grasping benchmarks such as Cornell~\cite{lenz2014deep} labeled graspable regions as 2D oriented rectangles, covering only top-down parallel-jaw approaches.
Dex-Net 2.0~\cite{mahler2017dex} used analytical force-closure synthesis to generate millions of grasp labels without physical trials.
GraspNet-1B~\cite{fang2020graspnet} annotated 1.2 billion 6-DoF grasps across cluttered real-world scenes, and GraspClutter6D~\cite{back2025graspclutter6d} extended this to denser clutter configurations.
For multi-fingered hands ($>$20 DoF), DexGraspNet 2.0~\cite{zhang2024dexgraspnet20learninggenerative} synthesized 427M grasps via diffusion-based generation (90.7\% real-world success), and Dex1B~\cite{ye2025dex1b} reached one billion grasps.
Tactile modalities complement vision: T-DEX~\cite{guzey2023tdex} showed that 2.5 hours of self-supervised tactile play data improves dexterous task success by 1.7$\times$ over vision-only baselines, capturing slip and reorientation signals invisible to cameras.
Beyond grasping, Omnipush~\cite{bauza2021omnipush} records planar pushing dynamics across 250 objects, PartNet-Mobility~\cite{xiang2020sapien} provides part-level interaction data for articulated objects, and FurnitureBench~\cite{heo2023furniturebench} supplies 5,000 teleoperated assembly trajectories with a paired simulator.
Language-grounded grasp datasets that bridge skill-level and task-level supervision are discussed in \S\ref{sec:task_datasets_narrative}.

\subsubsection{Task-level Datasets}\label{sec:task_datasets_narrative}

Building on skill-level primitives, task-level datasets record complete multi-step manipulation trajectories which often paired with natural language instructions. Table~\ref{tab:real_world_tasks} lists representative datasets along this tier.

MT-Opt~\cite{kalashnikov2021mt} collected 800k trajectories through autonomous exploration but used task IDs to index actions, without language supervision. 
BC-Z~\cite{jang2022bc} introduced language-conditioned supervision by pairing 26k trajectories with 100 semantic verbs, and RT-1 Dataset~\cite{brohan2023rt1} extended this to 130k trajectories spanning 700+ instructions. 
These early datasets were collected in one or two institutional kitchens, limiting environmental diversity. 
BridgeData V2~\cite{walke2024bridgedatav2} widened scene coverage to 24 environments via multi-site collection, DROID~\cite{khazatsky2024droid} coordinated 18 institutions across 564 scenes with calibrated extrinsics, and Dobb-E~\cite{shafiullah2023bringing} accessed 22 domestic environments using only an iPhone mounted on a Stretch robot. 
FMB~\cite{fu2025fmb} added force-torque calibration across 22k contact-rich assembly trajectories. More recently, RoboMIND~\cite{wu2025robomind} included 5,000 failure demonstrations with annotated error causes among its 107k trajectories, and RoboMIND 2.0~\cite{hou2026robomind20} extends to 310k trajectories across 6 robots and 739 tasks.

\subsubsection{Foundation-level Datasets}\label{sec:foundation_datasets}

Foundation-level datasets aggregate trajectories across heterogeneous robot morphologies under standardized observation and action protocols. Table~\ref{tab:foundation_datasets} summarizes representative datasets along this tier. RoboNet~\cite{dasari2020robonet} collected 162k trajectories across seven robot platforms. OXE~\cite{padalkar2023open} unified over one million trajectories from 22 morphologies via the RLDS protocol. Aggregating heterogeneous data introduces observation misalignment across camera configurations and sensor setups; ARIO~\cite{wang2024ario} addresses this with approximately 3 million episodes featuring synchronized multi-view timestamps, and RH-20T~\cite{fang2023rh} integrates force-torque and audio signals across 110k sequences. AgiBot World Beta~\cite{agibot2025} provides over one million trajectories ($\sim$43.8 TB) from 100+ humanoid robots for bimanual and dexterous manipulation. Ego-Exo4D~\cite{grauman2024egoexo4d} provides 740 hours of synchronized first- and third-person human video across 8 activity domains, connecting human demonstrations with the exocentric viewpoints used by robotic systems.

\subsection{Data Acquisition}\label{sec:data_acquisition}

The dataset taxonomy above establishes what knowledge each tier provides; this subsection examines how that knowledge is acquired. Three channels supply training datasets: real-world gathering, simulation ecosystems, and automatic data generation. Together they populate the skill-level, task-level, and foundation-level regimes described in \S\ref{sec:eai_datasets}.

\subsubsection{Real-world Data Gathering}\label{sec:real_acquisition}

Real-world data gathering has evolved along a consistent direction: reducing the cost and physical constraints of collection while broadening the environments and embodiments covered. The progression can be understood through three shifts in how human effort is coupled to the robot.

The first shift was from co-located single-operator setups to distributed teleoperation. MIME~\cite{sharma2018mime} provided paired human-robot demonstrations in a single workspace, and RoboTurk~\cite{mandlekar2018roboturk} showed that remote operators connected over the internet could collect comparable-quality trajectories at lower marginal cost. ALOHA~\cite{zhao2023learning, fu2024mobile} reduced the hardware barrier for bimanual and mobile whole-body teleoperation, making it practical for smaller labs to collect contact-rich data. DROID~\cite{khazatsky2024droid} pushed this further by coordinating 18 institutions to gather 76k trajectories across 564 scenes with standardized protocols, demonstrating that distributed collection can produce datasets with both scale and environmental diversity.

The second shift decoupled the collection device from the deployment robot. UMI~\cite{chi2024umi} replaced the robot entirely during collection, using handheld grippers and consumer cameras for in-the-wild capture; the resulting trajectories are retargeted to the deployment platform offline. This removes the requirement that a robot be physically present during data gathering, which substantially lowers the cost of accessing diverse real-world environments.

The third shift moves the data source from robot manipulation to human activity. Egocentric human video (EgoMimic~\cite{kareer2025egomimic}) provides large-scale demonstrations without any robot hardware, though it introduces a retargeting gap between human hand morphology and robot end-effectors. The correction mechanisms that support autonomous collection loops are discussed in \S\ref{sec:correction_recovery}, and their pipeline-level instantiations in \S\ref{sec:data_factories_robotic_arms}.

\subsubsection{Simulation Data Ecosystems}\label{sec:simulation_infrastructure}

Simulation for manipulation is now organized around a few reusable ecosystems with distinct data emphases. Table~\ref{tab:sim_classification} compares their design priorities. MuJoCo centers contact-stable, controller-oriented dynamics; SAPIEN centers articulated-object interaction and part semantics; and NVIDIA Isaac centers GPU-parallel data generation at scale. Specialized platforms remain useful when the target is a narrower benchmark, a particular task family, or a physical regime not covered well by these larger ecosystems.

\end{table}

MuJoCo~\cite{todorov2012mujoco} is commonly used when contact stability and controller-oriented evaluation matter. Meta-World~\cite{yu2021meta} provided a standard state-based multi-task setting, and later suites such as LIBERO~\cite{liu2023libero} and RoboCasa~\cite{nasiriany2024robocasa} added lifelong-learning curricula and richer household scenes. MJX and MuJoCo Playground support larger-batch rollouts, differentiable simulation, and sim-to-real analysis within the same stack~\cite{deepmind2025playground}.

SAPIEN~\cite{xiang2020sapien} is used primarily for articulated-object interaction, where kinematic structure and part semantics must be represented explicitly. PartNet-Mobility provides articulated assets with annotated joints, ManiSkill builds benchmark suites on this basis~\cite{gu2023maniskill2}, and SIMPLER uses the same infrastructure for sim-to-real evaluation~\cite{li2025simpler}.

NVIDIA Isaac emphasizes throughput and large-batch synthesis. Isaac Lab provides the common runtime and tooling layer, while DexGraspNet and FurnitureBench illustrate how the ecosystem is used for dexterous grasp generation and high-DoF manipulation data at larger scale~\cite{mittal2023orbit, wang2023dexgraspnetlargescaleroboticdexterous, heo2023furniturebench}.

Beyond these three ecosystems, specialized platforms remain necessary. PyBullet and CoppeliaSim still support manipulation baselines such as RLBench and CALVIN~\cite{james2020rlbench, mees2022calvin}; Habitat and AI2-THOR support indoor semantic reasoning benchmarks such as ALFRED and OpenEQA~\cite{shridhar2020alfred, majumdar2024openeqa}; and Genesis targets deformable and multi-physics interaction, where rigid-body simulators remain incomplete~\cite{genesis2024}.

\subsubsection{Automatic Data Generation}\label{sec:auto_data_gen}

Automatic data generation builds on simulation ecosystems to produce scenes, demonstrations, and rollouts without manual effort. We organize this along three axes: environment construction, demonstration synthesis, and world-model rollouts.

Scene generation has moved from procedural randomization to semantically grounded synthesis. ProcTHOR~\cite{deitke2022procthor} generated large-scale procedural indoor environments, Holodeck~\cite{yang2024holodeck} added language-conditioned semantic control, and RoboGen~\cite{wang2024robogen} connected asset retrieval, scene construction, and policy training within one automated pipeline.

Demonstration synthesis has likewise progressed from trajectory augmentation to task-aware generation. MimicGen~\cite{mandlekar2023mimicgen} showed that one demonstration can be expanded into many valid trajectories through spatial transformation, and GenSim~\cite{wang2024gensim} moved this idea to task generation by using LLMs to produce simulation code and reward structure.

Generative world models extend automation by learning dynamics directly from data. Cosmos~\cite{nvidia2025cosmos} represents the internet-scale version of this idea, training video foundation models on web data to learn physical priors. IRASim~\cite{zhu2025irasim} grounds this in robot-specific interaction video, and DreamGen uses learned world models as both data generators and training environments~\cite{jang2025dreamge}. Their main limitation remains long-horizon physical consistency, which becomes critical once generated rollouts are used for planning or policy improvement.

\subsection{Data Refinement}\label{sec:data_refinement}

Once data can be collected or synthesized at scale, the bottleneck shifts from availability to quality. We organize data refinement around four mechanisms: visual and viewpoint augmentation (\S\ref{sec:trajectory_redrawing}) expands the perceptual distribution, physically grounded augmentation (\S\ref{sec:dynamics_constraints}) preserves executability under kinematic and contact constraints, correction and recovery augmentation (\S\ref{sec:correction_recovery}) supplies the failure-recovery data absent from expert-only datasets, and data curation (\S\ref{sec:data_refinement}) filters and reweights heterogeneous datasets. 

\subsubsection{Visual and Viewpoint Augmentation}\label{sec:trajectory_redrawing}

Visual and viewpoint augmentation expands existing demonstrations along two main axes: semantic appearance variation and camera transformation, both without requiring new robot rollouts. Early work such as GenAug and ROSIE used generative image models to vary textures, objects, and scene composition in order to widen the visual distribution seen during imitation learning~\cite{chen2023genaug, yu2023scalingrobotlearningsemantically}. Later methods moved from appearance randomization to viewpoint-aware synthesis: RoVi-Aug augments both robot appearance and camera pose while preserving action labels and contact consistency under novel views~\cite{chen2025roviaug}. A complementary line extends supervision beyond robot demonstrations: VISTA enables zero-shot novel-view synthesis for view-invariant policy learning, H2R converts egocentric human video into robot-centric pretraining data by replacing hands with rendered end-effectors, and LucidSim uses generative visuals coupled with physics simulation to synthesize first-person data for RGB sim-to-real transfer~\cite{wang2024vista, li2026h2r, yu2024lucidsim}.

\subsubsection{Physically Grounded Augmentation}\label{sec:dynamics_constraints}

Augmenting physical trajectories (e.g., shifting a grasp 5\,cm laterally) requires that the result still obey kinematic limits, collision constraints, and contact dynamics. CP-Gen~\cite{lin2025cpgen} integrates kinematic boundaries with contact dynamics for one-shot trajectory augmentation. Physics-Driven Data Generation~\cite{yang2025physicsdriven} combines VR human demonstrations, retargeting, and trajectory optimization to maintain dynamic feasibility across embodiments. CyberDemo~\cite{wang2024cyberdemo} transfers simulated human demonstrations through large-scale in-simulator augmentation to real-world dexterous policies with minimal real data. Beyond geometric feasibility, RoCoDA~\cite{ameperosa2025rocoda} uses symmetry to distinguish perturbations that should leave the policy invariant from those that should induce equivariant action changes.

\subsubsection{Correction and Recovery Augmentation}\label{sec:correction_recovery}

Expert-only datasets lack failure-recovery examples, leaving policies brittle at out-of-distribution states. DMD~\cite{zhang2024dmd} uses diffusion models to synthesize corrective observations from perturbed states, enabling DAgger-style recovery without physical collection. Complementing these offline methods, IntervenGen~\cite{pinto2024intervengen} and CR-DAgger~\cite{xu2025compliant} systematize online human intervention during autonomous rollouts, converting sparse operator corrections into dense recovery-aware training signal.

\subsubsection{Data Curation and Verification}\label{sec:data_cureation}

As datasets scale to millions of heterogeneous trajectories, the bottleneck shifts from quantity to quality. Existing methods operate at three main granularities: dataset-level filtering or reweighting to suppress noisy and suboptimal demonstrations, as in Re-Mix~\cite{hejna2025remix}; segment- or transition-level pruning to remove redundancy and preserve informative or long-tail samples, as in SCIZOR~\cite{zhang2025scizor}; and policy-aware valuation methods that score data by downstream utility, as in CUPID~\cite{agia2025cupidcuratingdatarobot}. 

\subsection{Data Pipeline}\label{sec:data_factories_robotic_arms}

The acquisition, synthesis, and refinement methods described above are increasingly integrated into end-to-end production systems. Data pipelines automate the flow from raw collection through augmentation, curation, and verification, treating data production as an engineering stack with tightly coupled stages. This integration is critical as manipulation research scales: manual coordination across simulators, real robots, and curation tools becomes prohibitively expensive, and systematic biases introduced at any stage propagate through the entire learning pipeline.

RoboTwin~\cite{mu2025robotwin} and RoboTwin v2~\cite{chen2025robotwin2} keep synthetic data tethered to measured physical setups by matching dual-arm kinematics and sensor noise; the second version adds MLLM-driven task synthesis for automatic scenario generation. RoboVerse~\cite{geng2025roboverse} addresses fragmentation across simulators and datasets by providing a standardized MetaSim backbone spanning many tasks and assets. At larger scale, AgiBot World Colosseo~\cite{agibot2025} couples fleet-scale humanoid collection with simulator-based augmentation so that real and synthetic streams remain mutually useful, while NVIDIA Isaac GR00T\footnote{\url{https://developer.nvidia.com/isaac/gr00t}} packages synthetic data generation, foundation-model training, and sim-to-real deployment into a humanoid-oriented stack built on Isaac infrastructure. Supporting these pipelines, AIRSPEED~\cite{xia2024airspeed} decouples collection and generation from specific hardware backends, LeRobot~\cite{lerobot2024} turns fragmented real-world trajectories into shareable repositories, and MimicLabs~\cite{saxena2025matters} studies how quantity, diversity, and quality trade off as pipelines scale. These infrastructures move data production toward automated, reproducible workflows, but introduce new challenges in maintaining physical fidelity, cross-platform compatibility, and systematic quality control.

\section{Learning Paradigms of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:embodiment_method}

The data ecosystem of \S\ref{sec:embodiment_dataset} provides the raw material; the learning paradigms determine how that material is converted into manipulation capability. We decompose the learning stack into \emph{cognitive planning} (\S\ref{sec:embodied_brain}, the ``embodied brain''), which converts semantic intent into structured plans classified by output artifact (B1--B5), and \emph{policy learning} (\S\ref{sec:policy_learning_low_level}, the ``embodied cerebellum''), which maps observations to motor commands via three action-representation families (P1--P3). Section~\ref{sec:integration_models} then examines how these pathways are coupled into unified (Type~I), hierarchical (Type~II), or world-state-maintaining (Type~III) architectures; Table~\ref{tab:data_learning_map} traces how each data tier favors particular B/P/Type combinations. Finally, \S\ref{sec:deployment_pipeline} traces the deployment pipeline as an engineering concern that cuts across all three patterns. Figure~\ref{fig:learning_paradigms} illustrates the overall organization of learning paradigms.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=16cm 1cm 15cm 1cm,clip]{assets/learningparadigms.pdf}
  \caption{Organization of learning paradigms for embodied manipulation. The framework decomposes into cognitive planning (B1--B5), policy learning (P1--P3), and integration architectures (Type I--III), with connections to data tiers and deployment stages.}
  \Description{A diagram showing the organization of learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The top level shows cognitive planning methods (B1 through B5) ranging from semantic logic to generative dynamics. The middle level shows policy learning approaches (P1 through P3) covering explicit continuous, discrete action, and iterative generative methods. The bottom level shows integration architectures (Type I, II, III) that couple planning and policy. Arrows indicate connections between data tiers, learning paradigms, and deployment stages.}\label{fig:learning_paradigms}
\end{figure}

\subsection{Cognitive Planning: The Embodied Brain}\label{sec:embodied_brain}

Cognitive planning bridges the gap between abstract human intent and the concrete specifications that downstream controllers require. We classify planning methods into five paradigms (B1--B5) according to the primary artifact they produce for execution. B1 methods output semantic specifications such as executable code, reward functions, or skill rankings. B2 methods output logical task structures, decomposing long-horizon goals into ordered sub-task sequences or formal plans. B3 methods occupy an intermediate role: by jointly processing visual observations, robot state, and language within a shared backbone, they produce representations that are both semantically structured and physically situated, serving as the perceptual substrate from which B4 and B5 can derive more explicit spatial or predictive commitments. B4 methods output explicit spatial objects, including contact regions, SE(3) poses, affordance maps, and neural fields, that planners and controllers can consume directly. B5 methods output predicted future states, evaluating candidate actions by simulating their physical consequences before execution. Across these paradigms, a general trend is visible: planning representations become progressively more grounded in the physical world. This trend, however, should not be read as a strict pipeline; many systems combine artifacts from adjacent paradigms, and integrated architectures (\S\ref{sec:integration_models}) often invoke several within the same stack. Table~\ref{tab:brain_paradigms} summarizes the paradigm-level properties and representative methods.

\subsubsection{Semantic Logic and Code Generation}\label{sec:code_gen}

B1 methods convert human intent into executable semantic representations: code, reward functions, skill rankings, or structured control scripts. Early work framed this as probabilistic alignment between language and affordance; SayCan~\cite{brohan2023can} grounds abstract plans by scoring primitive skills against both semantic relevance and execution feasibility, but remains limited to atomic actions without compositional logic. Program synthesis goes further: CaP~\cite{liang2022code} generates executable Python from natural language, and ProgPrompt~\cite{singh2022progprompt} embeds precondition assertions directly into the prompt structure to enforce logical constraints. A parallel line generates objective functions and leaves motor optimization to downstream solvers; Language to Reward~\cite{tan2023l2r} synthesizes programmatic reward functions that decouple semantic task specification from continuous trajectory optimization. For structured control flow, LLM-as-BT~\cite{ao2025llmbt} generates modular behavior trees that support reactive switching and error recovery. Demo2Code~\cite{wang2023demo2code} extends the input modality by translating visual demonstrations into symbolic code, grounding program synthesis in physical observation alongside language. A shared limitation of B1 methods is that their outputs remain in the semantic layer and do not engage with the geometric or dynamic structure of the physical world; execution quality depends entirely on the downstream skill API or solver.

\subsubsection{Long-Horizon Task Decomposition}\label{sec:task_decomp}

B2 methods structure long-horizon goals into ordered, dependency-aware sub-task sequences. A central challenge is that LLM-generated plans can be logically inconsistent over extended horizons. LLM+P~\cite{liu2023llmp} addresses this by translating natural language into PDDL and delegating reasoning to classical solvers that guarantee feasibility; L3M+P~\cite{agarwal2025l3mp} further enables the solver to learn domain transition models from experience, reducing reliance on pre-defined rules. Classical solvers, however, face combinatorial explosions in large environments. SayPlan~\cite{rana2023sayplan} mitigates this by anchoring search in 3D scene graphs, hierarchically pruning irrelevant regions before planning. Robustness requires closing the loop: Inner Monologue~\cite{huang2023inner} injects environmental feedback into the LLM context window for in-context replanning after execution failure. A complementary challenge is world-state maintenance across long horizons; Statler~\cite{yoneda2024statler} addresses this by coupling planning with explicit world-state memory, reducing errors caused by forgotten object states. B2 methods remain fundamentally symbolic: they depend on accurate world-state representations and degrade when perception is noisy or when physical effects are not captured by the symbolic model.

\subsubsection{Grounded Multimodal Reasoning}\label{sec:multimodal_reasoning}

B3 occupies an intermediate role between the symbolic outputs of B1/B2 and the explicit spatial or predictive commitments of B4/B5. They jointly process visual observations, robot state, and language within a shared foundation model backbone, producing representations that are both semantically structured and physically situated. These representations serve as the perceptual substrate from which downstream modules can derive spatial targets or predictive rollouts. PaLM-E~\cite{driess2023palme} injects continuous state vectors and visual patches directly into an LLM embedding space, enabling zero-shot referential reasoning over sensor inputs. RoboBrain~\cite{ji2025robobrain} complements this with structured knowledge retention, mining affordance-centric relationships to build a queryable semantic memory that reduces hallucination. EmbodiedGPT~\cite{mu2023embodiedgpt} extends grounded reasoning into the temporal domain through multimodal chain-of-thought, conditioning each reasoning step on the current visual observation while keeping the model tied to the scene at hand. More recently, ECoT~\cite{zawalski2025ecot} operationalizes this idea within VLA architectures by generating explicit intermediate reasoning traces, including task descriptions, object grounding, and step-level plans, before action prediction, demonstrating that such structured intermediate representations measurably improve policy performance. The limitation of B3 is that its outputs remain implicit representations. Downstream planners therefore need an additional grounding step before they can use these representations as spatial targets or predictive commitments.

  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=0.7\linewidth,trim=1cm 0cm 0cm 0.8cm,clip]{assets/brain.pdf}
  \caption{Cognitive planning paradigms for embodied manipulation. The figure summarizes five representative planning modes: semantic logic and code generation, long-horizon task decomposition, grounded multimodal reasoning, geometric grounding and spatial intelligence, and generative dynamics and physical imagination.}
  \Description{A diagram illustrating five cognitive planning paradigms for embodied manipulation. A central brain icon is surrounded by five labeled panels: semantic logic and code generation, long-horizon task decomposition, grounded multimodal reasoning, geometric grounding and spatial intelligence, and generative dynamics and physical imagination. The panels use a broccoli-into-pot task to show how each paradigm represents planning differently, from symbolic code and step decomposition to affordance maps, point clouds, and imagined future states.}\label{fig:brain_paradigms}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection{Geometric Grounding and Spatial Intelligence}\label{sec:affordance_mapping}

B4 methods convert semantic intent into explicit spatial objects that planners and controllers can consume directly: contact regions, object-centric motion parameters, affordance maps, neural fields, or geometric constraints. ManipLLM~\cite{li2024manipllm} predicts pixel-wise contact masks from language, and A3VLM~\cite{huang2024a3vlm} extends this grounding to articulated objects by inferring joint axes and motion types. In image space, CLIPort~\cite{shridhar2022cliport} establishes the two-stream formulation separating semantic understanding from spatial precision, while PASG~\cite{zhu2025pasg} strengthens the mapping with functional keypoints. In 3D, F3RM~\cite{shen2023distilled} injects open-vocabulary semantics into neural feature fields, while D3Fields~\cite{wang2025d3fields} extends this line with dynamic 3D descriptor fields that encode semantic features and instance masks and support goal specification from 2D images. VoxPoser~\cite{huang2023voxposer} and ReKep~\cite{huang2024rekep} push this line further by turning language or task descriptions into explicit value maps and time-varying keypoint constraints for downstream optimization. 
Across these methods, the common objective is to expose enough spatial structure for manipulation to query and optimize at execution time. 
Their limitation is the same: they model the current geometric state but do not predict what will happen after contact, leaving dynamic interaction and long-horizon consequences to other modules.

\subsubsection{Generative Dynamics and Physical Imagination}\label{sec:generative_dynamics}

B5 methods evaluate candidate actions by predicting future states, observations, or interaction outcomes before execution. One route uses learned predictive models as the substrate for imagination: UniPi~\cite{du2023unipi} reformulates policy as future video generation, UWM~\cite{zhu2025uwm} unifies video and action diffusion within one transformer, and V-JEPA2~\cite{assran2025vjepa} shifts from pixel reconstruction to latent predictive reasoning. Purely learned prediction, however, drifts from physical consistency over long horizons. A second line therefore constrains prediction with structural priors: Deep Lagrangian models~\cite{schulze2025lagrangian} inject analytical mechanics into learned dynamics, zhang et al.~\cite{zhang2025particlegrid} targets deformable interaction through particle-mesh structure, and LUMOS~\cite{nematollahi2025lumos} conditions predictive rollouts on language-level task compliance. Taken together, these methods define B5 by a shared commitment to evaluating action through predicted consequences, but they also expose its main difficulty: prediction becomes useful for planning only when it remains sufficiently stable under long-horizon physical interaction.

\subsection{Policy Learning: The Embodied Cerebellum}\label{sec:policy_learning_low_level}

Policy learning maps perceptual observations to motor commands. We organize methods into three families by how the action space is represented, not by whether sequence context or temporal structure is used internally. P1 (Explicit Continuous) policies output continuous action vectors via regression in a single forward pass; P2 (Discrete Action) policies quantize actions into tokens and generate them autoregressively via cross-entropy; P3 (Iterative Generative) policies model the full action distribution through multi-step denoising or flow matching. This distinction matters because it determines the expressiveness-latency trade-off: P1 is fastest but prone to mode averaging, P2 captures multimodal distributions at the cost of autoregressive latency, and P3 offers the richest distributional modeling but requires iterative refinement. Efficiency-oriented acceleration and compression are treated as a cross-cutting deployment concern in \S\ref{sec:deployment_pipeline}. Table~\ref{tab:policy_paradigms} summarizes the paradigm-level properties.

\begin{figure}[t]
  \centering
  \includegraphics[width=\linewidth,trim=0cm 0cm 0cm 0cm,clip]{assets/celeberrum.pdf}
  \caption{Comparison of policy learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The figure contrasts explicit continuous policies, discrete-action policies, and iterative generative policies in terms of distributional expressivity and computational complexity.}\label{fig:policy_paradigms}
  \Description{A comparison diagram of three policy learning paradigms for robotic manipulation. The figure is arranged along two axes: computational complexity increases from left to right, and distribution expressivity increases from top to bottom. The top row compares explicit continuous policies and discrete-action policies, while the bottom row illustrates iterative generative policies based on diffusion and flow matching.}
\end{figure}

\subsubsection{Explicit Continuous Policies}\label{sec:explicit_continuous_policies}

P1 policies map observations to continuous action vectors in a single forward pass, trading distributional expressiveness for inference speed. The basic design separates a pre-trained visual encoder from a lightweight action head: BC-Z~\cite{jang2022bc} uses this pattern for multi-task zero-shot generalization, MVP~\cite{xiao2022mvp} shows that masked visual pre-training yields representations robust enough to reduce the policy to a simple MLP, and PointPolicy~\cite{haldar2025pointpolicy} extends explicit regression to 3D point clouds. Single-step prediction, however, ignores trajectory context; Decision Transformer~\cite{chen2021decisiontransformer} reframes control as autoregressive sequence modeling with regression objectives, and the ACT line~\cite{zhao2023learning, fu2024mobile} uses action chunking and CVAE-based modeling to predict short-horizon sequences and reduce jitter in bimanual and mobile manipulation. BAKU~\cite{haldar2024baku} combines CNN-based feature extraction with a transformer policy for multi-task settings. To handle distribution shift, MimicPlay~\cite{wang2023mimicplay} learns goal-conditioned behaviors from play data, Iterative Residual Policy~\cite{chi2022iterativeresidualpolicy} predicts residual corrections to recover from execution drift, and Motion Tracks~\cite{ren2025motion_tracks} learns a unified motion representation for cross-embodiment adaptation. Regression-based policies remain prone to mode averaging when the demonstration data contains multiple valid responses for the same observation.

\subsubsection{Discrete Action Policies}\label{sec:discrete_action_policies}

P2 policies quantize continuous actions into discrete tokens and generate them autoregressively via cross-entropy, naturally capturing multimodal action distributions that unimodal regression cannot represent.
Early approaches employ uniform discretization: RT-1~\cite{brohan2023rt1} maps continuous dimensions to 256 bins, Gato~\cite{reed2022generalistagent} uses $\mu$-law encoding to tokenize diverse modalities into a unified sequence, and PerAct~\cite{shridhar2022peract} extends this to 3D by classifying actions over voxelized grids.
Fixed binning, however, forces a trade-off between precision and vocabulary size.
Learned quantization addresses this: BeT~\cite{shafiullah2022bet} defines action modes via k-means clustering, VQ-BeT~\cite{lee2024vqbet} learns compact codebooks through VQ-VAE, and CARP~\cite{gong2025carp} introduces coarse-to-fine hierarchical prediction.
Once actions are reliably tokenized, they can be generated within the same autoregressive framework used for text. VIMA~\cite{jiang2023vima} interleaves visual objects with text instructions for multimodal prompt conditioning, GR-1~\cite{wu2023gr1} unifies vision, language, and action in a single stream, Seer~\cite{tian2024seer} attributes much of the gain to the discrete formulation itself, and ARP~\cite{zhang2025arp} shows that chunk-level autoregressive generation generalizes across diverse robots. ICIL~\cite{fu2025icil} further treats imitation as sequence completion, adapting to unseen tasks by conditioning on demonstration prompts without gradient updates.
Discretization introduces two recurring costs: quantization limits action precision even with learned codebooks, and autoregressive decoding adds token-level latency that grows restrictive in high-frequency control.

\subsubsection{Iterative Generative Policies}\label{sec:iterative_generative_policies}

P3 policies model the full action distribution through multi-step denoising or flow matching, offering the richest distributional expressiveness at the cost of iterative refinement. Diffusion Policy~\cite{chi2023diffusion} adapts DDPMs to visuomotor control by learning the score function of the action distribution, avoiding the mode-averaging pitfalls of regression. Diffuser~\cite{janner2022diffuser} extends this to long-horizon planning by treating trajectory generation as probabilistic inpainting, StructDiffusion~\cite{liu2022structdiffusion} constructs physically valid multi-object configurations from language prompts, and DP3~\cite{ze2024diffusion} integrates point cloud processing to improve spatial grounding. Iterative denoising, however, incurs substantial inference latency.
Flow matching addresses this by learning velocity fields that transport noise to data along straighter trajectories with fewer integration steps. Consistency Policy~\cite{prasad2024consistency} distills a teacher diffusion model into few-step generation, Streaming Flow Policy~\cite{jiang2025streaming} streams action trajectories to the robot during generation, and RecFlow~\cite{xue2025recflow} accelerates inference by rectifying the learned flow itself.
At foundation-model scale, Octo~\cite{octomodelteam2024} trains a transformer-based diffusion policy on the Open X-Embodiment dataset for cross-embodiment manipulation, RDT-1B~\cite{liu2025rdtb} scales to the 1B-parameter regime on larger multi-robot data, and FLOWER~\cite{reuss2025flower} improves sample efficiency through flow-based acceleration.
P3 policies face a persistent trade-off between expressiveness and robustness: their generative flexibility can model complex action distributions but can also produce unstable or weakly executable trajectories, especially in contact-rich or out-of-distribution settings.

\subsection{Integration Models}\label{sec:integration_models}

After discussing cognitive planning and policy learning separately, we now consider how cognitive planning and policy learning are integrated into complete manipulation systems. RT-2~\cite{brohan2023rt2} showed that semantic understanding, reasoning, and action generation can be combined within a single model, opening a design space that has since developed along three architectural directions.

We classify integration architectures by their primary structural commitment rather than by model scale or empirical performance; systems with mixed characteristics are assigned to the type that best reflects their dominant design choice. Type~I emphasizes end-to-end unification within a shared backbone. Type~II introduces an explicit hierarchy between slow semantic reasoning and fast sensorimotor execution. Type~III maintains an explicit world state---spatial, predictive, or both---that is shared by planning and policy. These architectures differ mainly in how much intermediate structure is made explicit between observation and action, and in the computational and representational trade-offs that follow.

Integration architecture is also closely coupled with the available training data regime: skill-level data ($\mathcal{K}_1$) couples most naturally with Type~III architectures that maintain explicit spatial state, task-level data ($\mathcal{K}_2$) is well suited to Type~II architectures that separate semantic reasoning from execution, and foundation-level data ($\mathcal{K}_3$) favors Type~I architectures that maximize end-to-end learning and parameter sharing, while still leaving room for Type~III variants when spatial or predictive grounding is a primary modeling objective. Table~\ref{tab:data_learning_map} summarizes these correspondences, and Table~\ref{tab:embodied_models_2026_v4} surveys representative systems across the three types.

\subsubsection{Type I\@: Unified End-to-End Foundation Models}

Type I models treat actions as another modality tokenized alongside text and image patches, absorbing semantic understanding, reasoning, and action generation into a single backbone. Early systems discretize actions into language-like tokens: RT-2~\cite{brohan2023rt2} co-fine-tuned a vision-language model to output discrete action tokens, and OpenVLA~\cite{kim2024openvla} released a 7B model trained on 970k Open X-Embodiment trajectories. Continuous representations followed: $\pi_0$~\cite{black2024pi0} introduced flow matching for high-frequency dexterous control, and RDT-2~\cite{liu2026rdt2} extends this to bimanual manipulation with zero-shot cross-embodiment deployment. RoboMamba~\cite{liu2024robomamba} replaces self-attention with State Space Models for linear-time processing, and auxiliary reconstructive objectives encode 3D spatial structure to compensate for weak explicit geometry~\cite{song2025reconvla}.

The main trade-off in Type~I systems is between end-to-end unification and controllable execution. Their appeal lies in collapsing perception, semantic reasoning, and action generation into a shared representational pathway, which strengthens cross-modal transfer and reduces interface engineering. Their weakness is that semantic deliberation and motor control are forced to share the same architectural loop despite operating on different temporal and physical scales. GR00T N1~\cite{nvidia2025grootn1} makes this tension visible: it retains the ambition of a unified system, yet introduces a practical split between slower semantic processing and faster control-oriented action generation. This suggests that the challenge for Type~I is not only action representation, but how far unification can be pushed before real-time execution forces hierarchy back in.

\subsubsection{Type II\@: Hierarchical \& Modular Reasoning Models}

Type II models decouple slow semantic deliberation from fast sensorimotor execution. RT-H~\cite{belkhale2024rth} introduced language motions as an intermediate layer between high-level instructions and low-level actions. FiS-VLA~\cite{chen2025fis} coupled a slower semantic branch with a real-time execution branch to reduce control-side latency. CoT-VLA~\cite{zhao2025cotvla} and ThinkAct~\cite{huang2025thinkact} show that intermediate reasoning steps, whether expressed in natural language or visual tokens, improve long-horizon robustness. RoBridge~\cite{zhang2025robridge} places an explicit symbolic bridge between a high-level cognitive planner and an embodied agent. Hi Robot~\cite{shi2025hirobot} employs a System 1/System 2 hierarchical architecture to process open-ended instructions and real-time human feedback, HAMSTER~\cite{li2025hamster} targets cross-morphology transfer under mixed semantic-control pipelines, and GO-1~\cite{agibot2025} applies this hierarchical design to bimanual settings at industrial scale.

The key design question in Type~II is the form of the intermediate representation that links slow reasoning to fast execution. This representation must be expressive enough to preserve task-level structure, yet concrete enough for the control branch to consume without excessive decoding or ambiguity. Language-level intermediates remain human-interpretable but are often too coarse for reactive manipulation; chain-of-thought tokens preserve richer reasoning traces but increase latency with reasoning depth; and symbolic bridges improve compositionality only when their symbols can be grounded reliably in the scene and action space. The challenge for Type~II is therefore not simply to insert an interface, but to make that interface both interpretable and actionable.

\subsubsection{Type III\@: Spatial Grounding \& Predictive World Models}

Type III systems maintain an explicit world state that planning and policy jointly consume. This world state takes two forms. Spatial grounding systems build a persistent geometric representation: 3D-VLA~\cite{zhen20243dvla} embeds 3D perception into the LLM for spatial reasoning, SpatialVLA~\cite{qu2025spatialvla} grounds actions in metric 3D structure via Ego3D Position Encoding, TraceVLA~\cite{zheng2025tracevla} conditions generation on visual trajectory traces, and BridgeVLA~\cite{li2025bridgevla} aligns spatial and predictive representations to ensure kinematic feasibility. Predictive world models instead learn forward dynamics from data: GR-2~\cite{cheang2024gr2} trains action-conditioned video prediction at web scale, UWM~\cite{zhu2025uwm} unifies policy, forward dynamics, inverse dynamics, and video prediction in a single Diffusion Transformer, DreamZero~\cite{ye2026dreamzero} jointly models video and action as a World Action Model, and DreamVLA~\cite{zhang2025dreamvla} integrates a diffusion-based world model into the VLA pipeline for planning via imagined trajectories.

The key design question in Type~III is the form of the maintained world state. Spatial grounding provides precise geometric structure and supports verification, but degrades under occlusion, calibration error, and rapid scene changes. Predictive world models better capture temporal evolution, yet accumulate error over long horizons, so that trajectories that appear valid in rollout may become infeasible by execution time. The challenge for Type~III is therefore not simply to maintain explicit state, but to keep that state both physically faithful and execution-valid as the world evolves.

\subsection{Deployment Pipeline}\label{sec:deployment_pipeline}

Following these advances in embodied system design, the field has encountered a new set of challenges in deploying such systems on physical hardware. 
We discuss deployment in three stages: offline pre-deployment (\S\ref{sec:pre_deployment}), online physical alignment (\S\ref{sec:online_alignment}), and inference-time execution (\S\ref{sec:inference_execution}). 
These stages are analytically distinct but tightly coupled in practice: pre-deployment choices constrain later adaptation, and online alignment reshapes the execution properties that inference-time control must sustain. 
Table~\ref{tab:deployment_lifecycle} summarizes the main bottlenecks and representative techniques at each stage.

\subsubsection{Stage 1: Offline Pre-deployment}\label{sec:pre_deployment}

Before physical deployment, the primary bottleneck is bridging the sim-to-real gap and preparing models for cross-embodiment transfer. Q-Transformer~\cite{chebotar2023qtransformer} shows that autoregressive Q-functions can scale offline actor-critic learning to transformer policies through TD backups on static datasets. X-Sim~\cite{dan2025xsim} constructs embodiment-agnostic representations through a real-to-sim-to-real pipeline that establishes shared latent action spaces across platforms. ASAP~\cite{he2025asap} addresses automated physics alignment by learning a delta-action compensation model that corrects simulation-to-reality dynamics mismatch. As model size grows, pre-deployment compression also becomes necessary: FAST~\cite{pertsch2025fast} introduces DCT-based action tokenization, replacing per-dimension discretization with a frequency-space representation for more efficient deployment.

\subsubsection{Stage 2: Online Physical Alignment}\label{sec:online_alignment}

Once deployed on physical hardware, the primary bottleneck shifts from transfer preparation to adaptation under real contact, friction, sensing noise, and out-of-distribution objects. 
SimpleVLA-RL~\cite{li2026simplevlarl} shows that large VLA models can be fine-tuned directly through large-scale reinforcement learning, while DiWA~\cite{chandra2025diwa} adapts diffusion policies through an offline world model, reducing the amount of physical interaction needed for post-training improvement. 
ConRFT~\cite{chen2025conrft} combines offline BC/Q-learning with online consistency policies and human intervention checkpoints to support safe adaptation in contact-rich settings. Human-in-the-loop real-world reinforcement learning provides another effective route for this stage: HIL-SERL~\cite{luo2025humaninloop} combines demonstrations, learned reward modeling, and online human corrections to learn precise and dexterous manipulation skills directly on physical robots.
VT-Refine~\cite{huang2025vtrefine} further demonstrates that visuo-tactile policies, when paired with tactile digital twins and simulation-guided refinement, can improve high-precision dual-arm coordination. Other alignment strategies include residual adaptation via off-policy RL~\cite{xiao2025pld}. The adaptation choices made here directly affect Stage~3: aggressive full-parameter fine-tuning can improve task performance but complicate compression and serving, whereas residual or modular updates preserve more of the original policy structure at the cost of additional execution-time overhead.

\subsubsection{Stage 3: Inference-Time Execution}\label{sec:inference_execution}

During continuous deployment, the primary bottleneck becomes sustaining reliable closed-loop execution on edge hardware. QVLA~\cite{xu2026qvla} addresses action-sensitive quantization by allocating precision unevenly across channels, recognizing that action-critical dimensions tolerate less compression than language-oriented ones. PD-VLA~\cite{pdvla2025} accelerates inference by replacing autoregressive decoding with parallel fixed-point iterations over action chunks. Runtime robustness remains equally important: Policy Contrastive Decoding~\cite{wu2026policycontrastive} reduces spurious visual dependence during action generation, FAIL-Detect~\cite{xu2025detectfailures} monitors candidate failures under out-of-distribution states, and Neural MP~\cite{dalal2024neuralmp} couples learned policies with motion-planning-based safety constraints. Recovery-oriented execution further includes self-supervised runtime adaptation~\cite{hong2026rttp}.

\section{System Evaluation of Robotic Arms}\label{sec:evaluation}

Sections~\S\ref{sec:embodiment_hardware}--\S\ref{sec:embodiment_method} traced the chain from embodiment through data and learning to deployment. Evaluation closes that chain along four complementary axes: execution quality, data efficiency, generalization, and deployment cost. Table~\ref{tab:eval_metrics} summarizes representative metrics and protocols along these dimensions.
